model_response:
Sections 10 and 275 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 interact as follows:
 
According to Section 10, when an order is given under sections 359, 363, 365(10), 368, 377, or 378, the time limit for completing the assessment or reassessment is extended by an additional period of twelve months.

However, according to Section 275, if the draft of the proposed order of assessment referred to in section 275 is made at any time up to the time limit of assessment, reassessment or recomputation referred to in table 1, then the following conditions apply:

- The period excluding those specified in clause (a) of section 275 does not include the time taken in reopening the whole or any part of the proceeding on request of the assessee or in giving an opportunity to the assessee to be heard under section 244;
- The period excluding those specified in clause (b) of section 275 does not include the period commencing on the date on which stay on assessment proceeding was granted by an order or injunction of any court and ending on the date on which certified copy of the order vacating the stay was received by the jurisdictional principal commissioner or commissioner;
- The period excluding those specified in clause (c) of section 275 does not include the period commencing from the date on which the assessing officer intimates the central government or the prescribed authority that the contravention of the provisions of schedule III (table 23, 24, 25) or section 270(11)(i) has been withdrawn or rescinded, and ending with the date on which the copy of the order withdrawing the approval or rescinding the notification as the case may be.

Therefore, the interaction between sections 10 and 275 is as follows:

- If the draft of the proposed order of assessment referred to in section 275 is made before the end of the six-month period mentioned in section 359 or 363 or 365(10) or 368 or 377 or 378, the time limit for completing the assessment or reassessment is extended by an additional period of twelve months.
- However, the extension of the time limit is only applicable to the draft of the proposed order of assessment referred to in section 275, and not to the actual order of assessment.

instruction: Explain how Section 46 affects the application of Section 114.
context:
[Section 114 — Set off and carry forward of losses computed in respect of specified business]
Chapter: SET OFF, OR CARRY FORWARD AND SET OFF OF LOSSES

114. (1) Any loss, computed in respect of a specified business, referred to in section
			 46, shall be set off only against profits and gains of another specified business.
(2) Where for any tax year, loss computed in respect of a specified business cannot
be wholly set off under sub-section (1), so much of the loss not so set off or the whole
loss, as the case may be, shall be carried forward to the following tax year and—
       (i) be set off against the profits and gains, if any, of any specified business
           carried on by him for such tax year; and
       (ii) if the loss cannot be wholly so set off, the amount of loss not so set off
            shall be carried forward to the following tax year and so on.
Set off and carry forward of losses from specified activity.
gold_response:
Section 114 (Set off and carry forward of losses computed in respect of specified business) refers to Section 46 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

114. (1) Any loss, computed in respect of a specified business, referred to in section
			 46, shall be set off only against profits and gains of another specified business.
(2) Where for any tax year, loss computed in respect of a specified business cannot
be wholly set off under sub-section (1), so much of the loss not so set off or the whole
loss, as the case may be, shall be carried forward to the following tax year and—
       (i) be set off against the profits and gains, if any, of any specified business
           carried on by him for such tax year; and
       (ii) if the loss cannot be wholly so set off, the amount of loss not so set off
            shall be carried forward to the following tax year and so on.
Set off and carry forward of losses from specified activity.

Readers should also refer to Section 46 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 114 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 provides for setting off and carrying forward of losses from specified businesses. According to this section, any loss computed in respect of a specified business can be set off against profits and gains of another specified business.

However, there is an exception where the loss cannot be wholly set off. In such cases, the unsettable portion of the loss can be carried forward to the next tax year. This means that even if the loss cannot be fully set off against other specified businesses, it can still be carried forward to the next tax year.

In other words, Section 114 allows for partial set-off of losses, but also permits the carrying forward of unsettable portions of losses to subsequent tax years.

model_response:
Section 71 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) deals with the concept of "capital gains" and provides that where there is a change in the nature of an asset during the tax year, the entire capital gain shall be computed and reported in the same manner as if the asset had been held for more than one year.

In this specific scenario, the question asks about the effect of Section 71 on the application of Section 4 regarding the computation of capital gain when a capital asset is converted into stock-in-trade.

The correct explanation is as follows:

When a capital asset is converted into stock-in-trade, the entire capital gain is deemed to have taken place in the previous tax year. This means that the entire capital gain is computed and reported in the same manner as if the asset had been held for more than one year.

Therefore, the entire capital gain is not merely limited to the first year in which the asset was converted, but rather the entire gain is computed and reported in the same manner as if the asset had been held for more than one year. This implies that the entire capital gain is affected by the conversion of the asset into stock-in-trade, regardless of whether it takes place within the first year or multiple years.

This means that the conditions specified in Section 71 do not apply to the entire capital gain, but only to the portion of the capital gain that corresponds to the period during which the asset was converted into stock-in-trade. In other words, the entire capital gain is affected by the conversion, whereas only the portion corresponding to the period of conversion is subject to the conditions specified in Section 71.

- If a person makes a false statement in verification, delivers an account or document which is false, and it is discovered that the statement was false, then the person shall be punishable under Section 482.
- However, if the person has already filed his return of income, then the person shall be punishable under Section 294, even if the statement is false.

model_response:
Section 338 of the Income-tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) provides for the applicability of certain provisions of the Act to investments made by a registered non-profit organization.

According to Section 338, an investment made by a registered non-profit organization is considered "investment" for the purpose of Section 337. This means that the entire amount invested by the organization is treated as its "specified income".

However, there are some exceptions to this rule. For instance, if the investment is made in contravention of the provisions of Section 338(a), then only the amount that is actually invested is considered as specified income.

Additionally, if the investment is made in contravention of the provisions of Section 338(b), then only the amount that is actually accumulated or set apart is considered as specified income.

In general, the key point is that the entire amount invested by the organization is considered as specified income, subject to the condition that it is not accumulated or set apart for purposes other than charitable or religious purposes.

Therefore, according to Section 338, the entire amount invested by a registered non-profit organization is considered as its specified income, unless it is specifically excluded by virtue of Section 338(a) or (b).

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 243 (Power to transfer cases) and Section 241?
context:
[Section 243 — Power to transfer cases]
Chapter: TAX ADMINISTRATION

243.		(1) The specified income-tax authority may transfer any case from one or
						more Assessing Officers subordinate to him (whether with or without con-
current jurisdiction) to any other Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers (whether
with or without concurrent jurisdiction) also subordinate to him.
(2) If the Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers, from whom the case is to be
transferred and the Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers, to whom the case is to
be transferred are not subordinate to the same specified income-tax authority, and
the concerned specified income-tax authorities––
       (a) are in agreement, then the specified income-tax authority from whose
           jurisdiction the case is to be transferred may pass the order;
       (b) are not in agreement, the order transferring the case may be passed by
           the Board or any such specified income-tax authority as the Board may,
           by notification, specify in this behalf.
(3) The order of transfer under sub-section (1) or (2) may be passed by the specified
income-tax authority after giving the assessee a reasonable opportunity of being
heard, wherever it is possible to do so and after recording his reasons therefor.
(4) Nothing in sub-section (1) or (2) or (3) shall be deemed to require any such
opportunity of being heard to be given, where the transfer is from any Assessing
Officer or Assessing Officers (whether with or without concurrent jurisdiction) to
any other Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers (whether with or without concur-
rent jurisdiction) and the offices of all such officers are situated in the same city,
locality or place.
(5) The transfer of a case under sub-section (1) or (2) may be made at any stage of
the proceedings, and it shall not be necessary to re-issue any notice already issued
by the Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers from whom the case is transferred.
(6) For the purposes of section 241 and this section, “case”, in relation to any per-
son whose name is specified in any order or direction issued thereunder, means all
proceedings under this Act in respect of any year, which may be pending on the date
of such order or direction or which may have been completed on or before such
date, and includes also all proceedings under this Act which may be commenced
after the date of such order or direction in respect of any year.
(7) For the purposes of sections 241, 242 and this section, “specified income-tax
authority” means the Principal Director General or Director General or Principal
Chief Commissioner or Chief Commissioner or Principal Commissioner or
Commissioner.
Change of incumbent of an office.
gold_response:
Section 243 (Power to transfer cases) refers to Section 241 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

243.		(1) The specified income-tax authority may transfer any case from one or
						more Assessing Officers subordinate to him (whether with or without con-
current jurisdiction) to any other Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers (whether
with or without concurrent jurisdiction) also subordinate to him.
(2) If the Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers, from whom the case is to be
transferred and the Assessing Officer or Assessing Officers, to whom the case is to
be transferred are not subordinate to the same specified income-tax authority, and
the concerned specified income-tax authorities––
       (a) are in agreement, then the specified income-tax authority from whose
           jurisdiction the case is to be transferred may pass the order;
       (b) are not in agreement, the order transferring the case may be passed by
           the Board or any such specified income-tax authority as the Board may,
           by notification, specify in this behalf.

Readers should also refer to Section 241 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 243 and 241 is that they deal with the power to transfer cases between Assessing Officers. Specifically, Section 243 deals with the power to transfer cases from one Assessing Officer to another, while Section 241 deals with the process for passing orders related to transfers.

In essence, Section 241 provides the framework for how to initiate and conduct the transfer process, including the procedures for obtaining consent from the Assessor, providing the assessee with an opportunity to be heard, and recording the reasons for the transfer.

Section 243, however, specifies the conditions under which the transfer can take place, including the requirement for the Assessor's consent and the need for the Assessor to give the assessee a reasonable opportunity to be heard. It also outlines the circumstances under which the transfer can be made at any stage of the proceedings.

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 77 (Special provision for computation of capital gains in case of slump sale) and Section 41?
context:
[Section 77 — Special provision for computation of capital gains in case of slump sale]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

77. (1) Any profits or gains arising from the slump sale effected in the tax year
				 shall be chargeable to income-tax as long-term capital gains and shall be
deemed to be the income of the tax year in which the transfer took place, subject
to the provisions of sub-section (2).
(2) The profits and gains arising from a slump sale involving the transfer of a capital
asset, being one or more undertakings or divisions owned and held by an assessee
for thirty-six months or less, immediately before the date of its transfer, shall be
treated as short-term capital gains.
(3) In relation to capital assets, being an undertaking or division transferred by
way of slump sale,—
       (a) the “net worth” of the undertaking or division shall be deemed to be the
           cost of acquisition and the cost of improvement for sections 72 and 73;
           and
       (b) the fair market value of the capital assets on the date of transfer, calcu-
           lated in such manner, as may be prescribed, shall be deemed to be the
           full value of the consideration received or accruing as a result of such
           transfer.
(4) Every assessee, in the case of a slump sale, shall furnish in the prescribed form
a report of an accountant, before the specified date referred to in section 63, and
the report shall—
       (a) include the computation of the net worth of the undertaking or division;
           and
       (b) certify that the net worth has been correctly arrived at as per the provi-
           sions of this section.
(5) For the purposes of this section,—
       (a) the “net worth” shall be the “aggregate value of total assets” of the under-
           taking or division, as reduced by the value of its liabilities as appearing
           in the books of account, and for computing net worth, any change in the
           value of assets due to revaluation shall be ignored;
       (b) the “aggregate value of total assets” shall,—
             (i) for depreciable assets, be the written down value of the block of
                 assets determined under section 41(1)(c);
            (ii) for capital asset being goodwill of a business or profession, which
                 was not acquired by the assessee by purchase from a previous
                 owner, be nil;
           (iii) for capital assets for which the entire expenditure has been allowed
                 or is allowable as a deduction under section 46, be nil; and
            (iv) for other assets, be the book value.
Special provision for full value of consideration in certain cases.
gold_response:
Section 77 (Special provision for computation of capital gains in case of slump sale) refers to Section 41 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

77. (1) Any profits or gains arising from the slump sale effected in the tax year
				 shall be chargeable to income-tax as long-term capital gains and shall be
deemed to be the income of the tax year in which the transfer took place, subject
to the provisions of sub-section (2).
(2) The profits and gains arising from a slump sale involving the transfer of a capital
asset, being one or more undertakings or divisions owned and held by an assessee
for thirty-six months or less, immediately before the date of its transfer, shall be
treated as short-term capital gains.
(3) In relation to capital assets, being an undertaking or division transferred by
way of slump sale,—
       (a) the “net worth” of the undertaking or division shall be deemed to be the
           cost of acquisition and the cost of improvement for sections 72 and 73;
           and
       (b) the fair market value of the capital assets on the date of transfer, calcu-
           lated in such manner, as may be prescribed, shall be deemed to be the
           full value of the consideration received or accruing as a result of such
           transfer.

Readers should also refer to Section 41 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 77 and 41 is as follows:
- Both sections deal with the computation of "net worth" for the purpose of determining capital gains.
- Section 41 deals with the valuation of assets for depreciation purposes.
- Section 77 specifically provides for the treatment of slump sales involving the transfer of capital assets for a period of less than 36 months.
- Section 41 also deals with the valuation of assets for depreciation purposes.

Therefore, Section 77 provides special provisions for the computation of capital gains in case of slump sales, while Section 41 provides for the valuation of assets for depreciation purposes.

instruction: Explain how Section 32 affects the application of Section 44.
context:
[Section 44 — Amortisation of certain preliminary expenses]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

44.		 (1) If an assessee, being an Indian company or a person (other than a
			 company), who is resident in India, incurs any expenditure specified in
sub-section (2)—
      (a) before the commencement of its business; or
      (b) after the commencement of its business, in connection with the extension
          of its undertaking or in connection with its setting up a new unit,
the assessee shall be allowed a deduction of an amount equal to one-fifth of such
expenditure for each of the five successive tax years beginning with—
       (i) the tax year in which the business commences, for clause (a); or
      (ii) the tax year in which the extension of the undertaking is completed or
           the new unit commences production or operation, for clause (b).
(2) The expenditure referred to in sub-section (1) shall be—
      (a) the expenditure in connection with—
             (i) preparation of feasibility report;
            (ii) preparation of project report;
           (iii) conducting market survey or any other survey necessary for the
                 business;
           (iv) engineering services relating to the business;
      (b) legal charges for drafting any agreement between the assessee and any
          other person for any purpose relating to the setting up or conduct of the
          business;
       (c) in addition to expenditure in clauses (a) and (b), if the assessee is a
           company,—
             (i) legal charges for drafting and printing of the Memorandum and
                 Articles of Association of the company;
            (ii) fees for registering the company under the provisions of the Com-
                 panies Act, 2013 (18 of 2013);
           (iii) expenditure in connection with the issue, for public subscription,
                 of shares in or debentures of the company, being underwriting
                 commission, brokerage and charges for drafting, typing, printing
                 and advertisement of the prospectus; and
      (d) such other items of expenditure (not being expenditure eligible for any
          allowance or deduction under any other provision of this Act), as may
          be prescribed.
(3) In relation to expenditure specified in sub-section (2)(a), the assessee shall
furnish a statement containing the particulars of the expenditure in such form and
manner, as may be prescribed.
(4) The allowable deduction under sub-section (1) in respect of aggregate of expend-
iture referred to in sub-section (2) shall be restricted to 5%—
      (a) of the cost of the project; or
      (b) of the capital employed in the business of the company, where the assessee
          is an Indian company, at its option.
gold_response:
Section 44 (Amortisation of certain preliminary expenses) refers to Section 32 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

44.		 (1) If an assessee, being an Indian company or a person (other than a
			 company), who is resident in India, incurs any expenditure specified in
sub-section (2)—
      (a) before the commencement of its business; or
      (b) after the commencement of its business, in connection with the extension
          of its undertaking or in connection with its setting up a new unit,
the assessee shall be allowed a deduction of an amount equal to one-fifth of such
expenditure for each of the five successive tax years beginning with—
       (i) the tax year in which the business commences, for clause (a); or
      (ii) the tax year in which the extension of the undertaking is completed or
           the new unit commences production or operation, for clause (b).

Readers should also refer to Section 32 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 44 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) allows for the amortization of certain preliminary expenses incurred by an assessee, including companies and individuals, in connection with the extension of their business or the setting up of a new unit.

To understand how Section 44 affects the application of Section 32, we need to examine the relationship between these two sections.

Section 32 of the Act provides that an assessee can claim a deduction of 100% of the expenditure incurred in the first year of business, provided it is incurred within the first six months of the business. This means that the assessee can claim a full deduction of the entire expenditure incurred in the first year of business.

However, Section 44 allows for the amortization of certain preliminary expenses incurred in subsequent years. Specifically, it provides that an assessee can deduct an amount equal to one-fifth of the expenditure specified in Section 44 for each of the five successive tax years starting from the tax year in which the business commenced.

In other words, the assessee can amortize the expenditure specified in Section 44 over a period of five years, rather than claiming a full deduction in the first year of business.

This means that the assessee can benefit from both Sections 32 and 44, allowing them to claim a full deduction in the first year of business and then amortize the expenditure over the next four years. This dual benefit maximizes the overall tax savings for the assessee.

Therefore, the application of Section 44 has a significant impact on the applicability of Section 32, allowing the assessee to take advantage of both sections to maximize their tax benefits.

model_response:
Section 263 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) provides for the assessment of income of a non-resident Indian who has become resident in India due to changes in their circumstances.

According to Section 263, where a non-resident Indian becomes resident in India in a tax year, they are required to furnish a declaration to the Assessing Officer stating that the provisions of sections 212 to 218 shall continue to apply to them in relation to the investment income derived from any foreign exchange asset referred to in section 212(e) other than shares in an Indian company.

This means that when the non-resident Indian's foreign exchange assets are converted into Indian rupee, the provisions of sections 212 to 218 will still apply to their total income for that tax year, including the converted assets.

However, if the non-resident Indian chooses not to be governed by these provisions, they can declare in their return of income under section 263 that they do not want to be governed by sections 212 to 217 for that tax year. In this case, their total income for that tax year would be computed and charged to tax according to the other provisions of the Act.

Therefore, Section 263 effectively creates a "catch-all" provision that allows a non-resident Indian to opt out of being governed by sections 212 to 217 if they wish to avoid the provisions of those sections. This provision enables them to take advantage of the benefits of sections 212 to 217 while also avoiding the complexities and costs associated with them.

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 222 (Tax on income in case of venture capital undertakings) and Section 224?
context:
[Section 222 — Tax on income in case of venture capital undertakings]
Chapter: DETERMINATION OF TAX IN SPECIAL CASES

222.		(1) Irrespective of anything contained in any other provision of this Act,
					where a person, out of investments made in a venture capital company or
venture capital fund, receives any income, or any income accrues or arises to him,
such income shall be chargeable to income-tax in the same manner as if it were the
income accruing or arising to, or received by, such person, had he made investments
directly in the venture capital undertaking.
(2) The person responsible for crediting or making payment of the income on behalf
of a venture capital company or a venture capital fund and the venture capital com-
pany or venture capital fund shall furnish, within such time, as may be prescribed,
to the person who is liable to tax in respect of such income and to the prescribed
income-tax authority, a statement in the prescribed form and verified in the pre-
scribed manner, giving details of the nature of the income paid or credited during
the tax year and such other relevant details, as may be prescribed.
(3) The income paid or credited by the venture capital company and the venture
capital fund shall be deemed to be of the same nature and in the same proportion in
the hands of the person referred to in sub-section (1) as it had been received by, or
had accrued or arisen to, the venture capital company or the venture capital fund,
as the case may be, during the tax year.
(4) The provisions of Chapter XIX-B shall not apply to the income paid by a venture
capital company or venture capital fund under this Chapter.
(5) The income accruing or arising to or received by the venture capital company
or venture capital fund during a tax year from investments made in venture capital
undertaking, if not paid or credited to the person referred to in sub-section (1), shall
be deemed to have been credited to the account of the said person—
       (a) on the last day of the tax year; and
       (b) in the same proportion in which such person would have been entitled
           to receive the income had it been paid in the tax year.
(6) Any income which has been included in total income of the person referred to
in sub-section (1) in a tax year, on account of it having accrued or arisen in the said
tax year, shall not be included in the total income of such person in the tax year in
which such income is actually paid to him by the venture capital company or the
venture capital fund.
(7) Nothing contained in this section shall apply in respect of any income accruing
or arising to, or received by, a person from investments made in a venture capital
company or venture capital fund, being an investment fund specified in section
224(10)(a).
(8) For the purposes of this section, “venture capital company”, “venture capital
fund” and “venture capital undertaking” shall have the meanings respectively
assigned to them in Schedule V (Note 4).
Tax on income of unit holder and business trust.
gold_response:
Section 222 (Tax on income in case of venture capital undertakings) refers to Section 224 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

222.		(1) Irrespective of anything contained in any other provision of this Act,
					where a person, out of investments made in a venture capital company or
venture capital fund, receives any income, or any income accrues or arises to him,
such income shall be chargeable to income-tax in the same manner as if it were the
income accruing or arising to, or received by, such person, had he made investments
directly in the venture capital undertaking.
(2) The person responsible for crediting or making payment of the income on behalf
of a venture capital company or a venture capital fund and the venture capital com-
pany or venture capital fund shall furnish, within such time, as may be prescribed,
to the person who is liable to tax in respect of such income and to the prescribed
income-tax authority, a statement in the prescribed form and verified in the pre-
scribed manner, giving details of the nature of the income paid or credited during
the tax year and such other relevant details, as may be prescribed.

Readers should also refer to Section 224 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 222 and 224 is that they deal with the taxation of income accruing or arising to or received by a person from investments made in a venture capital company or venture capital fund.

Specifically, Section 222 deals with the taxation of income paid by a venture capital company or venture capital fund to its investors, while Section 224 deals with the taxation of income accruing or arising to or received by a person from investments made in a venture capital company or venture capital fund.

In other words, Section 222 provides for the charging of tax on income paid by a venture capital company or venture capital fund to its investors, whereas Section 224 deals with the charging of tax on income accruing or arising to or received by a person from investments made in a venture capital company or venture capital fund.

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 76 (Debenture) and Section 72?
context:
[Section 76 — Debenture]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

76. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 2(101) or section 72, the
		 gains on the transfer or redemption or maturity, of a capital asset as mentioned
in sub-section (2) shall be treated as short-term capital gains and shall be computed
as per sub-section (3).
(2) For the purposes of sub-section (1), the capital asset shall be—
       (a) a unit of a Specified Mutual Fund acquired on or after the 1st April, 2023
           or a Market Linked Debenture; or
       (b) an unlisted bond or an unlisted debenture which is transferred or
           redeemed or matures on or after the 23rd July, 2024.
(3) For the purposes of sub-section (1), the short-term capital gains shall be computed
as per the following formula:—
		 X = A – B – C,
		where,—
		 X = short-term capital gains;
		 A = full value of consideration received or accruing as a result of
   the transfer or redemption or maturity of the debenture or unit or
   bond;
		 B = the cost of acquisition of the debenture or unit or bond; and
		 C = the expenditure incurred wholly and exclusively in connection
   with such transfer or redemption or maturity.
(4) In computing capital gains under sub-section (3), no deduction shall be allowed
for any sum paid as securities transaction tax as per Chapter VII of the Finance
(No. 2) Act, 2004 (23 of 2004).
(5) For the purposes of this section,—
       (a) “Market Linked Debenture” means a security, by whatever name called,
           which has an underlying principal component in the form of a debt
           security and where the returns are linked to market returns on other
           underlying securities or indices, and include any security classified or
           regulated as a market linked debenture by the Securities and Exchange
           Board of India;
       (b) “Specified Mutual Fund” means a Mutual Fund, by whatever name
           called, which invests more than 65% of its total proceeds in debt and
           money market instruments or a fund which invests 65% or more of its
           total proceeds in units of such Mutual Fund, subject to the following:—
             (i) the percentage of investment in debt and money market instruments
                 or in units of a fund shall be computed with reference to the annual
                 average of the daily closing figures;
            (ii) “debt and money market instruments” shall include any securities,
                 by whatever name called, classified or regulated as debt and money
                 market instruments by the Securities and Exchange Board of India.
Special provision for computation of capital gains in case of slump sale.
gold_response:
Section 76 (Debenture) refers to Section 72 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

76. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 2(101) or section 72, the
		 gains on the transfer or redemption or maturity, of a capital asset as mentioned
in sub-section (2) shall be treated as short-term capital gains and shall be computed
as per sub-section (3).
(2) For the purposes of sub-section (1), the capital asset shall be—
       (a) a unit of a Specified Mutual Fund acquired on or after the 1st April, 2023
           or a Market Linked Debenture; or
       (b) an unlisted bond or an unlisted debenture which is transferred or
           redeemed or matures on or after the 23rd July, 2024.
(3) For the purposes of sub-section (1), the short-term capital gains shall be computed
as per the following formula:—
		 X = A – B – C,
		where,—
		 X = short-term capital gains;
		 A = full value of consideration received or accruing as a result of
   the transfer or redemption or maturity of the debenture or unit or
   bond;
		 B = the cost of acquisition of the debenture or unit or bond; and
		 C = the expenditure incurred wholly and exclusively in connection
   with such transfer or redemption or maturity.

Readers should also refer to Section 72 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 76 (Debenture) and 72 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) is that they deal with the concept of "capital gains" and provide guidelines for their computation.

Section 72 of the Act deals with the concept of "capital gains", which includes gains from the sale, exchange, or transfer of certain types of assets, including debentures and mutual funds.

Section 76 (Debenture) specifically addresses the computation of capital gains related to debentures, providing detailed rules and formulas for calculating these gains.

In essence, Section 72 sets the stage for the calculation of capital gains, while Section 76 provides specific guidelines for debenture-related capital gains.

instruction: How do Sections 74 and 2 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 interact with each other?
context:
[Section 74 — Irrespective of anything contained in section 2(101), for a capital asset
			 forming part of a block of assets on which]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

74. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 2(101), for a capital asset
			 forming part of a block of assets on which depreciation has been allowed under
the Indian Income-tax Act, 1922 (11 of 1922) or under the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43
of 1961) or under this Act, the provisions of sections 72 and 73 shall be subject to
the provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3).
(2) If, during the tax year, the full value of consideration received or accruing for the
transfer of one or more assets in a block of assets exceeds the total of the following:—
       (a) expenditure incurred wholly and exclusively in connection with such
           transfer;
       (b) the written down value of the block of assets at the start of the tax year;
           and
       (c) the actual cost of any asset falling within the block of assets acquired
           during the tax year,
such excess shall be deemed to be capital gains arising from the transfer of short-
term capital assets.
(3) If any block of assets ceases to exist for the reason that all the assets in that block
are transferred during the tax year, then,—
       (a) the cost of acquisition of the block of assets shall be the written down
           value of the block of assets at the beginning of the tax year, as increased
           by the actual cost of any asset falling within that block of assets, acquired
           by the assessee during the tax year; and
       (b) the income received or accruing as a result of such transfer or transfers
           shall be deemed to be capital gains arising from the transfer of short-
           term capital assets.
Special provision for cost of acquisition in case of depreciable asset.
gold_response:
Section 74 (Irrespective of anything contained in section 2(101), for a capital asset
			 forming part of a block of assets on which) refers to Section 2 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

74. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 2(101), for a capital asset
			 forming part of a block of assets on which depreciation has been allowed under
the Indian Income-tax Act, 1922 (11 of 1922) or under the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43
of 1961) or under this Act, the provisions of sections 72 and 73 shall be subject to
the provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3).
(2) If, during the tax year, the full value of consideration received or accruing for the
transfer of one or more assets in a block of assets exceeds the total of the following:—
       (a) expenditure incurred wholly and exclusively in connection with such
           transfer;
       (b) the written down value of the block of assets at the start of the tax year;
           and
       (c) the actual cost of any asset falling within the block of assets acquired
           during the tax year,
such excess shall be deemed to be capital gains arising from the transfer of short-
term capital assets.

Readers should also refer to Section 2 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Sections 74 and 2 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 interact as follows:
- Section 74 deals with the effect of depreciation on capital assets.
- Section 2 of the Act defines 'capital asset' and provides that it includes property held for sale, exchange, or otherwise disposal for consideration.
- The interaction between Section 74 and Section 2 is as follows:
- Section 74 specifically mentions that if the full value of consideration received or accruing for the transfer of one or more assets in a block of assets exceeds the total of the expenditures incurred wholly and exclusively in connection with such transfer, etc., then the excess amount is considered as capital gains arising from the transfer of short-term capital assets.
- This means that even if an asset is being depreciated, the excess amount over the total expenditures incurred is still considered as capital gains, provided it does not exceed the threshold limit specified in Section 74.

instruction: Explain how Section 2 affects the application of Section 153.
context:
[Section 153 — Deduction for interest on deposits]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

153.		(1) An assessee who is—
       (a) an individual, not being a senior citizen; or
       (b) an individual, being a senior citizen; or
       (c) a Hindu undivided family,
shall be allowed a deduction from the gross total income, subject to conditions
specified in sub-section (2), where it includes income by way of interest on deposits
with—
       (i) a banking company to which the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 (10 of
           1949), applies (including any bank or banking institution referred to in
           section 51 of that Act); or
       (ii) a co-operative society engaged in carrying on the business of banking
            (including a co-operative land mortgage bank or a co-operative land
            development bank); or
      (iii) a Post Office as defined in section 2(d) of the Post Office Act, 2023
            (43 of 2023).
(2) The deduction under sub-section (1) shall be allowed for a tax year as follows:—
       (a) in case of an assessee mentioned in sub-section (1)(a) or (c), the whole of
           the interest up to a maximum amount of ` 10000 on deposits in a savings
           account, excluding time deposits;
       (b) in case of an assessee mentioned in sub-section (1)(b), the whole of the
           interest up to a maximum amount of ` 50000 on deposits in any account,
           including time deposits.
(3) Where the income referred to in sub-section (2)(a) is derived from any deposit
in a savings account held by, or on behalf of, a firm, an association of persons or
a body of individuals, no deduction shall be allowed under this section in respect
of such income in computing the total income of any partner of the firm or any
member of such association or any individual of such body of individuals.
(4) Where the income referred to in sub-section (2)(b) is derived from any deposit
held by, or on behalf of, a firm, an association of persons or a body of individuals, no
deduction shall be allowed under this section in respect of such income in computing
the total income of any partner of the firm or any member of such association or
any individual of such body of individuals.
(5) For the purposes of this section, the expression “time deposits” means the
deposits repayable on expiry of fixed periods.
                               E.—Other deductions
Deduction in case of a person with disability.
gold_response:
Section 153 (Deduction for interest on deposits) refers to Section 2 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

153.		(1) An assessee who is—
       (a) an individual, not being a senior citizen; or
       (b) an individual, being a senior citizen; or
       (c) a Hindu undivided family,
shall be allowed a deduction from the gross total income, subject to conditions
specified in sub-section (2), where it includes income by way of interest on deposits
with—
       (i) a banking company to which the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 (10 of
           1949), applies (including any bank or banking institution referred to in
           section 51 of that Act); or
       (ii) a co-operative society engaged in carrying on the business of banking
            (including a co-operative land mortgage bank or a co-operative land
            development bank); or
      (iii) a Post Office as defined in section 2(d) of the Post Office Act, 2023
            (43 of 2023).

Readers should also refer to Section 2 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 153 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) allows for the deduction of interest on certain types of deposits, specifically banking companies, co-operative societies, and post offices. 

The key aspect of Section 153 is its interaction with Section 2 of the Act, which defines "assessee" and outlines the conditions under which it can claim the deduction.

According to Section 2, an assessee is defined as:
"(a) an individual, not being a senior citizen; or
(b) an individual, being a senior citizen; or
(c) a Hindu undivided family."

Now, let's analyze how these definitions affect the application of Section 153:

- **Definition of Senior Citizen:** According to Section 2(1)(a), an individual who is not a senior citizen is eligible for the deduction under Section 153. This implies that individuals who do not meet the definition of a senior citizen may still qualify for the deduction if they have significant interest income from their banking accounts.

- **Definition of Senior Citizen:** On the other hand, Section 2(1)(b) defines a senior citizen as an individual who is 60 years old or more. Therefore, individuals who are 60 years old or more would not be eligible for the deduction under Section 153.

- **Hindu Undivided Family:** Section 2(1)(c) defines a Hindu undivided family as one whose members hold shares or interests in a trust or association. Since the definition does not explicitly mention age, we cannot conclude that only individuals meeting the age criterion qualify for the deduction. However, since the Act primarily focuses on the financial aspect rather than social status, it is likely that the focus remains on the financial aspect, making it unlikely that individuals belonging to Hindu undivided families would qualify for the deduction unless they also fall into the senior citizen category.

Therefore, considering the above explanations, the most accurate interpretation of how Section 2 affects the application of Section 153 is that only individuals who are not senior citizens (as per Section 2(a)) are eligible for the deduction under Section 153, while those who are senior citizens (as per Section 2(b)) are excluded.

instruction: How do Sections 127 and 154 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 interact with each other?
context:
[Section 127 — An assessee being an individual or a Hindu undivided family, who is a
					resident in India, shall be allowed a deducti]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

127.		(1) An assessee being an individual or a Hindu undivided family, who is a
					resident in India, shall be allowed a deduction up to ` 75000 from his gross
total income of a tax year, subject to the provisions of this section, if during that
year he has—
        (a) incurred expenditure for the medical treatment (including nursing),
            training and rehabilitation of a dependant, being a person with disability;
            or
       (b) paid or deposited any amount under a scheme framed by the Life
           Insurance Corporation or any other insurer or the Administrator, or the
           specified company, for the maintenance of a dependant, being a person
           with disability, subject to the conditions specified in sub-section (2) and
           approved by the Board in this behalf.
(2) The deduction under sub-section (1)(b) shall be allowed only if the following
conditions are fulfilled:—
       (a) the scheme referred to in sub-section (1)(b) provides for payment of an
           annuity or lump sum amount for the benefit of a dependant, being a
           person with disability—
             (i) on the death of the individual or the member of the Hindu undivided
                 family, in whose name the scheme was subscribed; or
             (ii) on attaining the age of sixty years or more by such individual or
                  the member of the Hindu undivided family, and the payment or
                  deposit to such scheme has been discontinued;
       (b) the assessee nominates the dependant, being a person with disability or
           any other person or a trust to receive the payments on behalf of and for
           the benefit of such dependant.
(3) If the dependant as referred to in sub-section (1) is a person with severe disability,
the amount of deduction as referred to in sub-section (1) shall be substituted with
“` 125000” for “` 75000”.
(4) In the event of death of the dependant, being a person with disability, before the
individual or the member of the Hindu undivided family mentioned in sub-section
(2), the amount paid or deposited under sub-section (1)(b) shall be deemed to be the
income of the assessee of the tax year in which it is received and shall accordingly
be chargeable to tax.
(5) The provisions of sub-section (4) shall not apply to the amount received by the
dependant, being a person with disability, before his death, as an annuity or lump
sum by application of the condition referred to in sub-section (2)(a)(ii).
(6) The assessee claiming deduction under this section, shall furnish a copy of the
medical certificate issued by the medical authority in such form and manner as
may be prescribed, along with the return of income under section 263 for the tax
year in which the deduction is claimed.
(7) If the certificate referred to in sub-section (6), specifies that the condition of
disability requires reassessment of its extent after a period stipulated in it, the
deduction under this section shall not be allowed for any tax year succeeding the tax
year in which the said certificate expires, unless a new certificate is obtained from
the medical authority in such form and manner as may be prescribed, and a copy
thereof is submitted along with the return of income under section 263.
(8) The dependant mentioned in this section shall not include a person who has
claimed deduction under section 154 in computing his total income for the tax year.
gold_response:
Section 127 (An assessee being an individual or a Hindu undivided family, who is a
					resident in India, shall be allowed a deducti) refers to Section 154 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

127.		(1) An assessee being an individual or a Hindu undivided family, who is a
					resident in India, shall be allowed a deduction up to ` 75000 from his gross
total income of a tax year, subject to the provisions of this section, if during that
year he has—
        (a) incurred expenditure for the medical treatment (including nursing),
            training and rehabilitation of a dependant, being a person with disability;
            or
       (b) paid or deposited any amount under a scheme framed by the Life
           Insurance Corporation or any other insurer or the Administrator, or the
           specified company, for the maintenance of a dependant, being a person
           with disability, subject to the conditions specified in sub-section (2) and
           approved by the Board in this behalf.

Readers should also refer to Section 154 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Sections 127 and 154 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 interact as follows:

- Section 127 allows deductions up to ₹75,000 per annum for certain expenses related to dependent persons, including those with disabilities.
- Section 154 allows deductions for certain expenses related to disabled persons, but excludes individuals who have already claimed deductions under Section 127.

In essence, Section 154 does not directly conflict with Section 127, as it specifically excludes individuals who have already claimed deductions under Section 127. However, there is a potential overlap between the two sections when considering claims for both Section 127 and Section 154.

For example, an individual might claim deductions under both Section 127 and Section 154 for the same dependent person. In such cases, the individual would need to provide documentation to support their claim, including a copy of the medical certificate from the medical authority for Section 127 and a separate document stating that they have already claimed deductions under Section 154 for the same dependent.

To resolve this overlap, the individual would typically need to file a joint return under Section 80-umbrella scheme, where they can claim deductions under both sections simultaneously. This would involve filing a single return with the necessary documentation and paying taxes at the end of the tax year.

instruction: Explain how Section 72 affects the application of Section 42.
context:
[Section 42 — Capitalising impact of foreign exchange fluctuation]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

42. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in any other provision of this Act,
			 where at the time of making payment during the tax year, there is a variation
in liability of an assessee as expressed in Indian currency, due to change in rate of
exchange, in relation to an asset acquired for the purpose of business or profes-
sion from a country outside India, it shall be dealt with in the manner specified in
sub-sections (2) and (3).
(2) For this section, the liability shall exclude any part met directly or indirectly
by any other person or authority and the “variation in liability” shall be computed
as—
     A=B–C
     where,—
		 A = variation in liability;
		 B = payment expressed in Indian currency at the time when it is made—
               (a) towards the whole or part of the cost of asset; or
               (b) towards repayment of the whole or part of the moneys borrowed,
                   directly or indirectly, along with interest in foreign currency,
                   specifically for acquiring such asset;
		 C = liability, corresponding to the amount referred in B, in Indian cur-
   rency at the time of acquisition of such asset.
(3) The variation in liability shall be added or reduced from the—
       (a) actual cost of the asset as referred in section 39; or
       (b) expenditure of capital nature referred to in section 32(i) or 45(1)(a)
           (i); or
       (c) cost of acquisition of a capital asset (not being a capital asset referred
           to in section 74) for the purpose of section 72,
and the amount arrived at after such addition or deduction shall be taken to be the
actual cost of the asset or the amount of expenditure of a capital nature or, as the
case may be, the cost of acquisition of the capital asset.
(4) Where the assessee has entered into a contract with an authorised dealer as
defined in section 2 of the Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 (42 of 1999),
for providing him with a specified sum in a foreign currency on or after a stipu-
lated future date at the rate of exchange specified in the contract to enable him
to meet the whole or any part of the said liability, the amount, if any, to be added
to, or deducted from, the actual cost of the asset or the amount of expenditure of
a capital nature or, as the case may be, the cost of acquisition of the capital asset
under this section shall, in respect of so much of the sum specified in the contract
as is available for discharging the said liability, be computed with reference to the
rate of exchange specified therein.
Taxation of foreign exchange fluctuation.
gold_response:
Section 42 (Capitalising impact of foreign exchange fluctuation) refers to Section 72 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

42. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in any other provision of this Act,
			 where at the time of making payment during the tax year, there is a variation
in liability of an assessee as expressed in Indian currency, due to change in rate of
exchange, in relation to an asset acquired for the purpose of business or profes-
sion from a country outside India, it shall be dealt with in the manner specified in
sub-sections (2) and (3).
(2) For this section, the liability shall exclude any part met directly or indirectly
by any other person or authority and the “variation in liability” shall be computed
as—
     A=B–C
     where,—
		 A = variation in liability;
		 B = payment expressed in Indian currency at the time when it is made—
               (a) towards the whole or part of the cost of asset; or
               (b) towards repayment of the whole or part of the moneys borrowed,
                   directly or indirectly, along with interest in foreign currency,
                   specifically for acquiring such asset;
		 C = liability, corresponding to the amount referred in B, in Indian cur-
   rency at the time of acquisition of such asset.

Readers should also refer to Section 72 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 72 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) deals with the "cost of acquisition of a capital asset". According to Section 42, if an asset's value increases due to changes in the exchange rate, the variation in liability should be calculated using the formula:
A = B - C
Where:
- A = Variation in liability
- B = Payment expressed in Indian currency at the time of making the payment
- C = Liability corresponding to the amount referred in B, in Indian currency at the time of acquisition of the asset.

However, Section 72 also provides that the variation in liability can be added or subtracted from either the actual cost of the asset or the expenditure of a capital nature. This means that the calculation of variation in liability under Section 42 can be affected by the cost of acquisition of the capital asset under Section 72.

In other words, if the cost of acquisition of the capital asset is less than the variation in liability, the variation in liability will be added to the cost of acquisition. Conversely, if the cost of acquisition is greater than the variation in liability, the variation in liability will be subtracted from the cost of acquisition.

Therefore, the effect of Section 72 on the application of Section 42 is that it allows for the inclusion of the cost of acquisition of a capital asset in calculating the variation in liability, which can affect the final outcome of the calculation.

model_response:
Sections 14 and 157 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 interact as follows:
- Section 14 deals with the computation of total income, specifically dealing with the concept of "earnings" and how it is calculated.
- Section 157 deals with the provision of deductions related to certain types of payments, including gratuity and compensation received during retirement.

In essence, sections 14 and 157 work together to determine the total income of an individual. According to section 14, the total income is calculated by adding up the cash equivalents of earnings received over a specific period, which includes both earned leave and non-earned leave. This calculation is then adjusted according to the provisions outlined in section 157, where certain types of payments are exempted or deducted from the total income.

For example, when calculating the total income, section 14 considers the cash equivalent of earnings received, which includes the value of the leave paid to the employee. However, certain types of payments, such as gratuity and compensation received during retirement, are exempted or deducted from the total income according to section 157. Therefore, sections 14 and 157 work together to ensure that the total income is accurately computed.

instruction: Explain how Section 263 affects the application of Section 83.
context:
[Section 83 — Where an assessee, being an individual or a Hindu undivided family,—
       (a) has capital gains arising from the trans]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

83. (1) Where an assessee, being an individual or a Hindu undivided family,—
       (a) has capital gains arising from the transfer of a capital asset, being land,
           which was used by the assessee or his parent, or the Hindu undivided
           family for agricultural purposes (original asset), in two years immediately
           preceding the date of transfer; and
       (b) has, within two years after that date, purchased any other land for being
           used for agricultural purposes (new asset),
then, instead of the capital gains being charged to income-tax as income of the tax
year in which the transfer took place, it shall be dealt with as follows:—
       (i) if the capital gains exceed the cost of the new asset, such excess shall be
           charged under section 67, and for computing any capital gains arising
           from the transfer of the new asset within three years of its purchase, the
           cost shall be nil; or
       (ii) if the capital gains is equal to or less than the cost of the new asset, no
            capital gains shall be charged under section 67, and for computing any
            capital gains arising from the transfer of the new asset within three years
            of its purchase, the cost shall be reduced by the amount of the capital
            gains.
(2) If the capital gains referred to in sub-section (1) is not utilised by the assessee to
purchase the new asset before filing the return of income under section 263, then—
       (a) the unutilised amount shall be deposited in a specified bank or institution
           and utilised as per the scheme notified by the Central Government;
       (b) such deposit shall be made before the filing of the return and not later
           than the due date applicable in the case of the assessee for filing the
           return of income under section 263(1); and
       (c) the proof of deposit shall be submitted along with such return.
(3) For the purposes of sub-section (1), the amount already utilised for purchasing
the new asset together with the deposited amount under sub-section (2), shall be
deemed to be the cost of the new asset.
(4) If the amount deposited under sub-section (2) is not fully utilised for purchase
of the new asset within the period specified in sub-section (1), then,—
       (a) the unutilised amount shall be charged under section 67 as the income
           of the tax year in which two years from the date of the transfer of the
           original asset expires; and
       (b) the assessee shall be entitled to withdraw such unutilised amount in
           accordance with the scheme referred to in sub-section (2).
Capital gains on compulsory acquisition of lands and buildings not to be
charged in certain cases.
gold_response:
Section 83 (Where an assessee, being an individual or a Hindu undivided family,—
       (a) has capital gains arising from the trans) refers to Section 263 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

83. (1) Where an assessee, being an individual or a Hindu undivided family,—
       (a) has capital gains arising from the transfer of a capital asset, being land,
           which was used by the assessee or his parent, or the Hindu undivided
           family for agricultural purposes (original asset), in two years immediately
           preceding the date of transfer; and
       (b) has, within two years after that date, purchased any other land for being
           used for agricultural purposes (new asset),
then, instead of the capital gains being charged to income-tax as income of the tax
year in which the transfer took place, it shall be dealt with as follows:—
       (i) if the capital gains exceed the cost of the new asset, such excess shall be
           charged under section 67, and for computing any capital gains arising
           from the transfer of the new asset within three years of its purchase, the
           cost shall be nil; or
       (ii) if the capital gains is equal to or less than the cost of the new asset, no
            capital gains shall be charged under section 67, and for computing any
            capital gains arising from the transfer of the new as

Readers should also refer to Section 263 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 263 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) deals with the filing of returns of income by individuals and Hindu undivided families. According to this section, if an individual or a Hindu undivided family purchases new land within two years of the transfer of the old land, they can choose between two options:
(i) Utilise the excess capital gains exceeding the cost of the new asset to buy the new land, and
(ii) Use the entire capital gains to buy the new land.

However, if the capital gains are utilised to buy the new land before filing their return of income, then the unutilised amount will be deposited in a specified bank or institution and utilised as per the scheme notified by the Central Government. This means that the unutilised amount will be utilized towards buying the new land, rather than being kept aside for future use.

Therefore, Section 263 does not directly affect the application of Section 83, but it provides a separate option for individuals and Hindu undivided families to utilize the excess capital gains to buy new land, thereby reducing the overall cost of acquiring new assets.

Time limit for completion of assessment, reassessment and recomputation.
model_response:
According to Section 283, if a notice under Section 280 can be issued at any time for the purpose of making an assessment or re-assessment or recomputation in consequence of, or to give effect to, any finding or direction contained in an order passed by any authority, including a Tribunal or Court, then the provisions of sub-section (1) do not apply.

Therefore, the tax implications under Section 283 would be:

* No assessment or re-assessment or recomputation can be made without a valid notice under Section 280.
* Any finding or direction contained in an order passed by any authority cannot be reopened after it has been made.

instruction:
A taxpayer faces a situation where its
          acceptance by the person is in violation of any law or rule or reg. What are the tax implications under Section 34?
context:
[Section 34 — General conditions for allowable deductions]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

34. (1) Any expenditure (not being an expenditure of the nature specified in
				 sections 28 to 33, 44 to 49, 51 and 52 and not being in the nature of capital
expenditure or personal expenses of the assessee), laid out or expended wholly
and exclusively for the purposes of the business or profession shall be allowed in
computing the income chargeable under the head “Profits and gains of business or
profession”.
(2) For the purposes of sub-section (1), an expenditure laid out or expended wholly
and exclusively for business or profession by the assessee shall not include any of
the following:—
      (a) an expenditure incurred for any purpose which is an offence or is pro-
          hibited by law; or
      (b) an expenditure incurred on the activities relating to corporate social
          responsibility referred to in section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013
          (18 of 2013); or
       (c) an expenditure incurred on advertisement in any souvenir, brochure,
           tract, pamphlet or the like, published by a political party.
(3) The expenditure mentioned in sub-section (2)(a) shall include expenditure
incurred for—
      (a) any purpose which is an offence under, or is prohibited by, any law in
          force in or outside India; or
      (b) providing a benefit or perquisite in any form to a person, who may or
          may not be carrying on a business or exercising a profession, when its
          acceptance by the person is in violation of any law or rule or regulation
          or guideline governing the conduct of that person; or
       (c) compounding an offence under any law in force in or outside India; or
      (d) settling proceedings initiated in relation to contravention under any law
          notified by the Central Government in this behalf.
Amounts not deductible in certain circumstances.
gold_response:
Under Section 34 (General conditions for allowable deductions) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, the following provisions apply:

34. (1) Any expenditure (not being an expenditure of the nature specified in
				 sections 28 to 33, 44 to 49, 51 and 52 and not being in the nature of capital
expenditure or personal expenses of the assessee), laid out or expended wholly
and exclusively for the purposes of the business or profession shall be allowed in
computing the income chargeable under the head “Profits and gains of business or
profession”.
(2) For the purposes of sub-section (1), an expenditure laid out or expended wholly
and exclusively for business or profession by the assessee shall not include any of
the following:—
      (a) an expenditure incurred for any purpose which is an offence or is pro-
          hibited by law; or
      (b) an expenditure incurred on the activities relating to corporate social
          responsibility referred to in section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013
          (18 of 2013); or
       (c) an expenditure incurred on advertisement in any souvenir, brochure,
           tract, pamphlet or the like, published by a political party.
model_response:
The taxpayer's acceptance of the person in violation of any law or rule or regulation or guideline governing the conduct of that person would result in deduction of the entire amount of such expenditures as provided under clause (b) of Section 34.

Therefore, the total income of the taxpayer would be reduced by the sum of all expenditures incurred by it for the purpose of the business or profession which are not deductible under Section 34.

instruction: How does Section 350 apply when a taxpayer falls under Section 350?
context:

Chapter: PROCEDURE FOR ASSESSMENT

350.		(1) The modes of investing or depositing the money under this Part, shall be
						such as specified in Schedule XVI.

                                      5. —Violations
Specified violation.
gold_response:
Under Section 350 (Permitted modes of investment) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, the following provisions apply:

350.		(1) The modes of investing or depositing the money under this Part, shall be
						such as specified in Schedule XVI.

                                      5. —Violations
Specified violation.
model_response:
According to Section 350, "A person who is required to file an income tax return under this Act shall be deemed to have made an investment for the purpose of this Part if he has deposited any amount of money with a bank, post office, or other financial institution."

Therefore, when a taxpayer falls under Section 350, they are considered to have made an investment for the purpose of this Part, even if they did not specify a particular mode of investment in their return. This means that the taxpayer's entire deposit of money can be considered as an investment for the purposes of Section 350, regardless of how it was received.

instruction: How does Section 111 apply when for any tax year?
context:
[Section 111 — Carry forward and set off of loss from Capital gains]
Chapter: SET OFF, OR CARRY FORWARD AND SET OFF OF LOSSES

111. (1)(a) Where for any tax year, loss computed under the head “Capital gains”
			 cannot be wholly set off against the income under the head “Capital gains”
as per section 108, so much of the loss not so set off or the whole loss, as the case
may be, shall be carried forward to the following tax year and shall be set off in the
following manner—
       (i) if such loss relates to a short-term capital asset, it shall be set off only
           against the income under the head “Capital gains”, if any, assessable for
           that tax year in respect of any other capital asset;
       (ii) if such loss relates to a long-term capital asset, it shall be set off only
            against the income under the head “Capital gains”, if any, assessable for
            that tax year in respect of any other long-term capital asset; and
(b) if the loss cannot be wholly so set off under clause (a), the amount of loss not so
set off shall be carried forward to the following tax year and so on.
(2) No loss shall be carried forward under this section for more than eight tax years
immediately succeeding the tax year for which the loss was first computed.
Carry forward and set off of business loss.
gold_response:
Under Section 111 (Carry forward and set off of loss from Capital gains) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, the following provisions apply:

111. (1)(a) Where for any tax year, loss computed under the head “Capital gains”
			 cannot be wholly set off against the income under the head “Capital gains”
as per section 108, so much of the loss not so set off or the whole loss, as the case
may be, shall be carried forward to the following tax year and shall be set off in the
following manner—
       (i) if such loss relates to a short-term capital asset, it shall be set off only
           against the income under the head “Capital gains”, if any, assessable for
           that tax year in respect of any other capital asset;
       (ii) if such loss relates to a long-term capital asset, it shall be set off only
            against the income under the head “Capital gains”, if any, assessable for
            that tax year in respect of any other long-term capital asset; and
(b) if the loss cannot be wholly so set off under clause (a), the amount of loss not so
set off shall be carried forward to the following tax year and so on.
(2) No loss shall be carried forward under this section for more than eight tax years
immediately succeeding the tax year for which the loss was first computed.
Carry forward and set off of business loss.
model_response:
According to Section 111, where the loss is not set off against "Capital gains" for a particular tax year, the unutilized portion of the loss can either be carried forward to the next tax year or set off against "Capital gains" for the same tax year.

In other words, if the loss is not set off against "Capital gains", it can be carried forward to the next tax year or used against "Capital gains" for the same tax year. This means that the unutilized portion of the loss can be utilized in subsequent tax years.
